results.possibleIdentity: Bill Williams is a Canadian-born lighting designer and technician who was a central figure in the New York City multimedia and experimental light show scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. After moving to New York to study lighting design at The Studio and Forum of Stage Design under Tom Skelton, Williams joined the Pablo Light Show (often referred to simply as "Pablo") in 1965. As a primary technician and designer for Pablo, he was instrumental in developing innovative projection techniques—such as the "amoeba" effect using watch glasses and overhead projectors—and specialized special effects equipment. Under the umbrella of Pablo, his work spanned theatre, television, fashion shows, and major rock concerts, providing visual support for many of the leading musical acts of the era at venues like the Fillmore East and the Electric Circus. His technical expertise in lighting and audio-visual systems led him to establish his own consulting firm, Bill Williams and Associates, in the early 1970s. He is widely recognized for his contributions to the history of the field, authoring "A History of Light and Lighting" and designing for hundreds of international stage and architectural projects. His work at the intersection of technology and art made him a likely collaborator or member within the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) community, which fostered similar partnerships between artists and engineers during his active years in New York.
